---
title: My dynamic report
output: html_fragment
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, error=FALSE)
library(tidyverse)
```

```{css}
html {background-color: #488A76 ; /* green page background */
}

body {background-color: transparent;
      font-family: 'Calibri', 'Arial', sans-serif;
      color: black;
      margin: 0;
      padding: 40px 20px;
}

/* Headings */ 
h1, h2 {color: white;
    font-size: 48px;
    text-align: center;
}

h2 {font-weight: 800;
    text-shadow: 3px 3px #1F3D31;
    margin-bottom: 10px;
}

h3 {color: white;
  font-weight: bold;
  margin-top: 5px;
  margin-bottom: 5px;
  text-align: center;

}

h4 {color: #1F3D31;
  font-size: 20px;
  margin-top: 5px;
  margin-bottom: 5px;
  text-align: left;
}

/* Text */ 
p {color: black;
  font-size: 15px;
  margin-bottom: 12px;
}

/* Highlight */ 
mark {background-color: #AFDEE3;
      color: #574240;
      padding: 2px 6px;
      border-radius: 5px;
 }

/* Content bubble */ 
.content-bubble {
    background-color: white;      /* white bubble */
    padding: 30px;                /* space inside the bubble */
    margin: 20px auto;            /* space outside and center horizontally */
    border-radius: 15px;          /* rounded corners */
    max-width: 1000px;            /* limit width */
    box-shadow: 0 4px 10px rgba(0,0,0,0.1);  /* shadow for 3D effect */
}

```

## Where Did All My Money Go? 💸
### A Week in Review
### by Hani Patel

<div class="content-bubble">
#### <mark> About this report </mark>
This dynamic report explores my personal spending over a week. It tracks every purchase I made, capturing:

* Amount spent – in dollars ($)  
* Category – the type of purchase (Food, Transport, Entertainment, Clothes, Bills, etc.)  
* Type of spending – whether it was a Need or a Want
* Mood while spending – how I felt at the time (Happy, Neutral, Bored, Stressed)  

The purpose of this analysis is to:

1. Identify which categories I spend the most on  
2. Compare spending between needs vs wants  
3. Explore whether mood might influence spending decisions  

All data is self-reported, anonymous, and used only for this assignment.
</div>

```{r}
# Load the data
# --------------------------------------------------------------------
logged_data <- read_csv("https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/e/2PACX-1vRNu8aT_HxKQM7yf0QDIQjH6pJv-iOtRLI6-8Vln53WhwrSZsgylpXsS1scO-4gdaKZbuQIbIxjOwpw/pub?output=csv")

# --------------------------------------------------------------------
# Rename variables for clarity and create latest_data
# --------------------------------------------------------------------
latest_data <- logged_data %>% 
  rename(timestamp = Timestamp,
         amount = `How much did you spend ($)?`,
         category = `What did you spend your money on?`,
         type = `What type of spending was this?`,
         mood = `Mood while spending?`)

# --------------------------------------------------------------------
# Summary values
# --------------------------------------------------------------------
# Numeric summaries
total_spent <- sum(latest_data$amount) %>% round(2)
average_spent <- mean(latest_data$amount, na.rm = TRUE) %>% round(2)
highest_single_purchase <- max(latest_data$amount, na.rm = TRUE) %>% round(2)

# Categorical summaries
most_common_category <- latest_data %>% 
                        count(category, sort = TRUE) %>%
                        slice(1) %>%
                        pull(category)

most_common_mood <- latest_data %>% 
                    count(mood, sort = TRUE) %>%
                    slice(1) %>%
                    pull(mood)
```

<div class="content-bubble">
#### <mark> Key spending summary 📌</mark>

In total, I spent $`r total_spent` over the week.

On average, each purchase was `r average_spent`, and my largest single purchase was $`r highest_single_purchase`.

The most common spending category was `r most_common_category`, and the mood I recorded most often was `r most_common_mood`.

</div>


<div class = "content-bubble">
#### <mark> Chart 1: How often did I spend in each category? </mark>
```{r}
# Chart 1: Number of Purchases by Category 
category_plot <- latest_data %>% 
      ggplot() +
      geom_bar(aes(x = category), fill = "#FD727B") +
      labs(title = "Number of Purchases by Category", 
              x = "Category", 
              y = "Number of Purchases",
              caption = "Source: Self-reported spending data")

print(category_plot)
```

</div>



<div class = "content-bubble">
#### <mark> Chart 2: Which categories cost me the most? </mark>
```{r}
# Chart 2: Total spending per category
category_spending_plot <- latest_data %>% 
        group_by(category) %>% 
        summarise(total_spent = sum(amount)) %>%
        ggplot (aes(x = category, y = total_spent)) +
        geom_col(fill = "#FD72C1") +
        labs(title = "Total Spending per Category", 
             x = "Category", 
             y = "Total Amount Spent ($)",
             caption = "Source: Self-reported spending data")

print(category_spending_plot)
```

</div>



<div class = "content-bubble">
#### <mark> Chart 3: What mood did I spend money in? </mark>
```{r}
# Chart 3: Number of Purchases by Mood
mood_plot <- latest_data %>% 
      ggplot() +
      geom_bar(aes(x = mood), fill = "#FDAE72") +
      labs(title = "Number of Purchases by Mood", 
             x = "Mood", 
             y = "Number of Purchases",
            caption = "Source: Self-reported spending data")

print(mood_plot)
```

</div>




<div class = "content-bubble">
#### <mark> Final takeaway 💭 </mark>
Overall, this report suggests that my spending was mainly focused on `r most_common_category`, and the mood I spent in most frequently was `r most_common_mood`.

</div>




![](https://c.tenor.com/-qBsG1HwR4oAAAAC/tenor.gif)
